name: Mae Nak Phra Khanong
alias: Nang Nak · the wife who would not leave
body: First it is the sight of an ordinary house lit warm against the canal dark, a woman at the doorway pounding rice, a child at her hip, the smell of cooking drifting over the water as though nothing in the world were wrong. Travellers along the Phra Khanong klong slow their boats to admire it. Only later, recounting the evening, do they remember that no man was ever seen there, that the lamps burned long after the village slept, and that the woman never once stepped beyond her own threshold into the light of the road.

Nak died in childbirth while her husband Mak was away at war, and she would not accept it. Love held her in the shape of a living wife: she kept the house, cooked the meals, and waited, and when Mak came home wounded she greeted him as if he had never been gone. The tell is domestic and terrible. One evening, preparing nam phrik on the raised floor of the house, she drops a lime through a gap in the boards, and her arm follows it down, stretching long and boneless to the earth below to fetch it back. To see a wife reach where no living arm can reach is to understand at last whose roof you are sheltered under, and that the bargain she offers, a home that never ends, is one you cannot survive accepting.

What lingers is the patience of her devotion, a hunger dressed as tenderness, the sense that being loved by the dead is its own kind of drowning. They say her rage, once exposed, scattered villagers across the rice fields and that only a holy man could bind her. To this day the faithful bring her cosmetics, dresses, and toys for the child she never raised, laid at the shrine attributed to her at Wat Mahabut in Phra Khanong, where the incense never quite stops burning and women still come to ask the most loyal ghost in Bangkok for luck in love.
note: The classic ending has the monk Somdet Phra Phutthachan (To Phromrangsi) subduing Nak and confining her spirit, in the best-known version by inscribing her power into a piece of her own skull bone worn as a waistband relic. Wards in the wider tradition are the standard ones against a malevolent ghost (phi): the prayers and sacred thread of an ordained monk, sanctified water, and yantra cloth. The tale is firmly local to the Phra Khanong district of Bangkok and is retold endlessly in Thai film, song, and theatre.

Whether Nak ever lived is unsettled; the story is usually dated to the reign of Rama IV or V in the nineteenth century. The shrine at Wat Mahabut remains an active site of devotion, where supplicants ask favours of a ghost remembered less for terror than for refusing to stop loving.
